query: does glucoraphanin from broccoli have any effect on body weight or obesity

retrieved_docs.title: Obesity Supplements -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: When evaluating the evidence for dietary weight-loss supplements, additional data are needed to understand each ingredient's efficacy, dosing, and safety. Human studies are limited, and data are often extracted from animal and laboratory research. Most human trials are small and of questionable quality, limiting interpretation. Many supplements marketed for weight loss contain multiple ingredients, making it impossible to isolate each ingredient's effect. Studies often use different formulations, combinations of ingredients, and variable dosages, all of which complicate the analysis of the supplement's safety and efficacy.

retrieved_docs.title: Obesity Supplements -- Function -- Beta-Glucans
retrieved_docs.content: Camellia sinensis is the active ingredient found in the green tea plant. This popular plant is thought to aid in weight loss through anti-lipidemic effects. [6] The data for human weight loss have been mixed, generally showing a modest effect. In a 2012 Cochrane systematic review, green tea was associated with a slight, statistically non-significant decrease in weight among overweight and obese adults. The mean difference in weight loss ranged from 0.2 kg to 3.5 kg compared with a placebo. [7] A recent meta-analysis found a statistically significant but modest decrease in body mass index (BMI) with green tea consumption. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Obesity Supplements -- Function -- Beta-Glucans
retrieved_docs.content: Beta-glucans are soluble fibers found in bacteria, yeast, fungi, and grains such as oat bran and barley. They are glucose polysaccharides fermented by the gut microbiota. [3] Weight loss effects may be due to increased satiety and decreased food intake. A meta-analysis published in 2019 showed that most trials reported non-significant or no weight loss from beta-glucans administered at a dose of 3 to 10 grams per day for 4 to 12 weeks. [4] Data does not currently support their use for the treatment of obesity. Adverse effects include increased flatulence. [5] Camellia Sinensis

retrieved_docs.title: Obesity Supplements -- Function -- Guar Gum
retrieved_docs.content: Hoodia gordonii is a succulent plant native to Africa. It contains a steroidal glycoside that acts centrally on the hypothalamus to suppress appetite. [53] While this popular supplement has been used for weight loss, the 1 randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled study examining H. gordonii found no significant improvement in energy intake, body weight, or fat percentage compared with a placebo. [54] Furthermore, adverse events were more common among those receiving H. gordonii , with altered skin sensation, headache, dizziness, and nausea among the most commonly reported symptoms. [54] Irvingia Gabonensis

retrieved_docs.title: Obesity Supplements -- Function -- Green Coffee Extract
retrieved_docs.content: Green coffee beans are unroasted mature or immature coffee beans. They are high in polyphenols, which have purported positive biologic effects, including anti-inflammatory activities and anti-diabetic, anti-lipidemic, and anti-hypertensive effects. [48] [49] While earlier studies showed inconsistent benefits of green coffee bean extract on weight and body mass, more recent studies have been more promising. A meta-analysis published in 2011 assessing the efficacy of green coffee extract on weight loss analyzed 3 human studies and found an overall moderate benefit in favor of green coffee extract versus placebo, with a mean weight loss of 2.5 kg after 4 to 12 weeks. [50] A study published in 2017 examined 64 obese women randomized to receive 400 mg of green coffee extract per day for 8 weeks and found significant reductions in body weight, body mass, and fat mass. [48] The safety of green coffee extract is difficult to assess due to the small sample size and duration of published studies. While none reported adverse events, more research is needed to detail a full safety profile. Given these promising early findings, green coffee extract is a topic for more extensive controlled clinical trials.

retrieved_docs.title: Obesity Supplements -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Americans spend over $2 billion a year on weight-loss dietary supplements. These supplements are costly and can contain dozens of ingredients, including plant components, herbs, dietary fiber, caffeine, and minerals. The proposed mechanisms of action vary for each supplement. These include blocking the absorption of fats and carbohydrates, reducing appetite and increasing satiety, increasing thermogenesis, and changing metabolism to improve body composition. There are concerns over the efficacy, safety, and purity of the compounds in supplements, which are usually marketed without substantiating data. The FDA regulates dietary supplements as foods, not drugs.

retrieved_docs.title: Obesity Supplements -- Function -- Fucoxanthin
retrieved_docs.content: Garcinia cambogia is a fruit-bearing tree native to Southeast Asia and India. The plant’s active compound, hydroxy citric acid (HCA), has several potential mechanisms for mediating weight loss. HCA is thought to decrease fatty acid synthesis and lipogenesis, suppress appetite by increasing serotonin availability in the brain, and increase hepatic glycogen synthesis, thus increasing satiety. In a 2011 meta-analysis, HCA decreased weight by 0.88 kg on average compared to a placebo. [38] While this finding did reach statistical significance, its clinical significance is questionable. Subsequent randomized controlled trials have failed to find a benefit. [39] [40] Furthermore, safety has been questioned due to reports of associated liver toxicity. While most of these cases occurred in patients taking combination products, 3 have occurred with Garcinia cambogia alone, and patients with liver disease should be cautioned. [41] [42]

retrieved_docs.title: Obesity Supplements -- Function -- Phaseolus Vulgaris /White Kidney Bean
retrieved_docs.content: Phaseolus vulgaris is a legume cultivated worldwide and marketed as a weight-loss supplement for its carbohydrate-blocking effects. Studies show that Phaseolus vulgaris inhibits pancreatic amylase, which decreases the gastrointestinal absorption of dietary starches. There have been several published clinical trials and reviews that show mixed results regarding weight loss efficacy. [60] [61] The authors of a 2011 systematic review of 6 trials found that 445 to 1,500 mg per day of P. vulgaris for 4 to 13 weeks significantly reduced body fat but not body weight. [62] A 2014 clinical trial of 123 overweight and obese participants consuming 3,000 mg a day of P. vulgaris with a hypocaloric diet over 12 weeks showed a modest yet significant reduction in body fat and body weight. This indicates that P. vulgaris has a possible modest effect on body fat and weight, but more data is needed to confirm this benefit. Reported adverse effects of P.vulgaris include headaches, soft stools, flatulence, and constipation. [63] No serious adverse effects of P.vulgaris have been reported. The long-term effects are unknown.

retrieved_docs.title: Pharmacologic Therapy for Obesity -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Clinicians must caution patients about the potential risks associated with weight-loss supplements and carefully monitor those who use them. The conclusions drawn from a 2015 review regarding some of these supplements are outlined below: Green tea, Garcinia cambogia, conjugated linoleic acid, and chitosan exhibited no significant impact on weight loss, indicating that healthcare professionals should not endorse their use. Data on the effectiveness of chromium, Gambisan, Hoodia gordonii , and Cynanchum auriculatum are inconclusive. Therefore, clinicians should refrain from recommending their use due to the lack of evidence and the potential risk of harm. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Obesity Supplements -- Function -- Fucoxanthin
retrieved_docs.content: Fucoxanthin is a carotenoid found in brown seaweed and other algae. Preclinical data suggest that fucoxanthin might effectively treat obesity through its anti-lipidemic properties. Only 2 clinical trials have been conducted using fucoxanthin. In a 16-week double-blind placebo-controlled trial, 151 non-diabetic, obese, pre-menopausal women with and without non-alcoholic fatty liver disease were given a combination of fucoxanthin with pomegranate seed oil (PSO) at different doses (300 mg algae containing 2.4 mg fucoxanthin plus 300 mg PSO versus 200 mg algae containing 1.6 mg fucoxanthin plus 200 mg PSO). [36] The group taking the higher dose of combined fucoxanthin plus PSO had a statistically significant reduction in body weight, waist circumference, and other metabolic parameters. In a double-blind, placebo-controlled trial of 31 obese participants receiving 0.8 mg fucoxanthin plus 100 mg of PSO versus placebo for 16 weeks, no significant differences in body fat or weight loss were observed. Toxicological assessments of the combined fucoxanthin and PSO extract administered to rats found no teratogenic or toxic effects. [37] Of note, a statistically significant decrease in body weight and food intake occurred in the treated rats. Based on the results of the published data, no recommendations can be made about the efficacy of fucoxanthin for overweight and obesity treatment in humans, but preclinical and clinical data show promise.

retrieved_docs.title: Obesity Supplements -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: People considering using weight-loss supplements should talk with their healthcare providers to discuss potential benefits and risks. This is especially important for patients with comorbid conditions who are taking prescription medications. Yet fewer than one-third of U.S. adults who use weight-loss dietary supplements discuss this with a clinician. [79] Healthcare providers should inquire about weight-loss supplements to facilitate open discussion. The evidence supporting the use of weight-loss dietary supplements is limited, inconclusive, and unconvincing. [80] [81] The benefits reported are often neither reproducible nor clinically significant. The safest, most effective way to lose weight is by choosing a healthy whole foods diet and obtaining regular physical activity. Clinicians can consider FDA-approved pharmaceuticals and bariatric procedures for patients requiring additional measures.

retrieved_docs.title: Epidemiologic and Etiologic Considerations of Obesity -- Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Large portion sizes significantly contribute to increased energy intake and obesity. Clinicians, coaches, and nutritionists can educate patients on proper portion sizes, calorie content, glycemic index of foods, and unique calorie needs based on physical activity levels. They may also dispel misconceptions about fad diets and teach strategies to avoid hunger-induced binge eating. Comprehensive weight management counseling should focus on reducing calorie intake and promoting healthier food choices. Patients who consume more low-fat, nutrient-dense foods, eg, fruits, vegetables, legumes, and whole grains, are more likely to meet their nutritional needs. Fresh vegetables, in particular, have high water content, adding weight and volume to meals, which enhances satiety without increasing calorie intake.

retrieved_docs.title: Pharmacologic Therapy for Obesity -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Human chorionic gonadotropin (hCG) has been promoted as a potential weight loss therapy; however, clinical trials do not support its efficacy. Several randomized trials have demonstrated that hCG injections or pills, when combined with a very low-calorie diet (500 kcal/d), are no more effective than a placebo in treating obesity. [6] The weight loss effect is solely correlated to caloric restriction. Notably, very low-calorie diets are not sustainable in the long term compared to conventional diets because people tend to regain weight rapidly upon discontinuing such diets, and their impact on other aspects of health remains unclear.

retrieved_docs.title: Obesity Supplements -- Function -- Glucomannan
retrieved_docs.content: Glucomannan appears to be well tolerated for short-term use. Minor adverse gastrointestinal effects include belching, bloating, frequent loose stools, flatulence, constipation, and abdominal discomfort. [46] There have been reports of esophageal obstruction following the consumption of glucomannan-containing compounds, specifically with the tablet formulation and in patients with esophageal pathology. The capsule form of the supplement has not been associated with this effect. [47]

retrieved_docs.title: Dietary Approaches to Obesity Treatment -- Clinical Significance -- Low Carbohydrate Diet
retrieved_docs.content: A low carbohydrate diet improves insulin sensitivity and benefits patients with diabetes, prediabetes, metabolic syndrome, overweight, and obesity. Long-term weight loss and health-promoting benefits of a low-carbohydrate, high-fat diet have not been demonstrated. The long-term sustainability of a low-carbohydrate, high-fat diet is also questionable. There is an abundance of data showing health-promoting and weight loss benefits of a relatively higher carbohydrate, plant-based, low glycemic-high fiber diet, which would be sustainable on a long-term basis. [20] A diet high in refined carbohydrates leads to frequent insulin spikes (secretory bursts), increased fat deposition, and lower satiety. A strict diet with low carbohydrates would also be poor in fiber and micronutrients. In addition, the higher fat content may also increase the intake of saturated fat, which may lead to adverse cardiovascular outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: Obesity Supplements -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Evaluate data on the efficacy of various dietary weight-loss supplements. Assess the mechanisms of action for dietary weight-loss supplements. Identify contraindications to common dietary weight-loss supplements. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2063
retrieved_docs.content: Peripherally acting antiobesity products are classified as a specific type of antiobesity preparations, excluding diet products. This subclass of antiobesity agents exerts its therapeutic effects through peripheral mechanisms rather than central pathways, distinguishing them from diet products and centrally acting weight‑loss medications. Recognizing that peripherally acting antiobesity products belong to the broader category of antiobesity preparations, but are not considered diet products, helps clinicians select appropriate pharmacologic options for obesity management based on mechanism of action and product classification.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2901
retrieved_docs.content: Mazindol and sibutramine are both classified as centrally acting antiobesity products, meaning they belong to a group of medications that exert their weight‑loss effects primarily through actions on the central nervous system. As members of this class, both agents work by modulating neurotransmitter pathways in the brain to suppress appetite and promote reduced caloric intake, which can be useful in the management of obesity. Recognizing mazindol and sibutramine as centrally acting antiobesity products highlights their shared mechanism of action and therapeutic role in weight‑control strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 2 -- Community 884
retrieved_docs.content: Centrally acting antiobesity products and peripherally acting antiobesity products are both classified as antiobesity preparations, excl. diet products. The centrally acting antiobesity products category includes the agents setmelanotide and sibutramine, which achieve weight loss through mechanisms that act within the central nervous system. Peripherally acting antiobesity products represent a separate class that exerts its effects outside the central nervous system, although specific members are not listed here. Understanding the distinction between central and peripheral mechanisms of antiobesity preparations helps clinicians choose appropriate pharmacologic strategies for obesity management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 2063
retrieved_docs.content: Glucagon-like peptide‑1 (GLP‑1) analogues constitute a class of therapeutic agents that mimic the incretin hormone GLP‑1, enhancing glucose‑dependent insulin secretion, inhibiting glucagon release, slowing gastric emptying, and promoting satiety. Liraglutide is a member of this GLP‑1 analogue class, and its pharmacologic actions make it valuable for the treatment of type 2 diabetes mellitus and for weight management in patients with obesity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 2147
retrieved_docs.content: The ATC classification system includes a category called OTHER ALIMENTARY TRACT AND METABOLISM PRODUCTS, within which the group ALIMENTARY TRACT AND METABOLISM DRUGS is classified. ALIMENTARY TRACT AND METABOLISM DRUGS encompasses antiobesity preparations, excl. diet products, indicating that these antiobesity agents are considered a subset of the broader alimentary tract and metabolism drug class. Within the antiobesity preparations, the subclass Peripherally acting antiobesity products is defined, representing agents that exert their weight‑loss effects through peripheral mechanisms rather than central nervous system pathways. This hierarchical organization clarifies how Peripherally acting antiobesity products are situated within the larger framework of antiobesity preparations, excl. diet products, and the overall ALIMENTARY TRACT AND METABOLISM DRUGS category in the ATC system.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 2 -- Community 762
retrieved_docs.content: Centrally acting antiobesity products and peripherally acting antiobesity products are both classified as antiobesity preparations, excl. diet products. The centrally acting antiobesity products category encompasses agents such as the bupropion / naltrexone combination and sibutramine, which exert their weight‑loss effects through central nervous system mechanisms. In contrast, peripherally acting antiobesity products represent a separate class that achieves antiobesity effects outside the central nervous system. Recognizing these mechanistic subclasses of antiobesity preparations helps clinicians select appropriate therapies based on the desired site of action and safety profile.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 1375
retrieved_docs.content: Fat emulsion and glucosamine are both classified as dietary supplements, indicating that each is used to augment nutrition or support specific health needs. As dietary supplements, they are intended to be taken in addition to regular food intake to provide additional nutrients or functional compounds.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 871
retrieved_docs.content: Centrally acting antiobesity products and peripherally acting antiobesity products are both classified as antiobesity preparations that exclude diet products. The centrally acting category comprises agents that exert their weight‑loss effects through central nervous system mechanisms, and it specifically includes the combination bupropion / naltrexone, the stimulant mazindol, and the melanocortin‑4 receptor agonist setmelanotide. In contrast, peripherally acting antiobesity products represent the other major subclass of antiobesity preparations, acting outside the central nervous system, although individual members of this subclass are not listed here. Recognizing the distinction between centrally and peripherally acting antiobesity products and their constituent drugs aids clinicians in selecting mechanisms‑based therapies for obesity management.

retrieved_docs.content: plant seeds and flowers). These ancillary ingredients are likely intended to supply nutritional support, antioxidant capacity, and additional pharmacologic actions that complement the primary adrenergic and corticotropic effects. The repeated pattern of pairing epinephrine with corticotropin, together with other endocrine hormones, neurotransmitters, minerals, vitamins, and herbal components, suggests a therapeutic strategy aimed at modulating sympathetic activity, adrenal cortical output, thyroid function, and overall metabolic homeostasis. The inclusion of both synthetic and natural substances across the products underscores a multimodal approach to patient (or animal) care that leverages synergistic actions of catecholamines, ACTH, and supportive nutrients to address conditions such as allergic reactions, neuro‑behavioral imbalances, weight management, and systemic endocrine deficiencies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 728
retrieved_docs.content: The ATC class “ALIMENTARY TRACT AND METABOLISM DRUGS” is positioned within the broader category “OTHER ALIMENTARY TRACT AND METABOLISM PRODUCTS in ATC,” indicating that it represents a specific subset of alimentary‑tract and metabolism agents. Within this class, “BILE AND LIVER THERAPY DRUGS” is a more specialized group that focuses on treatments for bile‑related and hepatic conditions; this group is itself part of the larger “ALIMENTARY TRACT AND METABOLISM DRUGS” hierarchy. “BILE AND LIVER THERAPY DRUGS” is further related to “liver therapy, lipotropics,” which are considered its parent therapeutic category. A distinct branch of the hierarchy concerns anti‑obesity agents. The subclass “antiobesity preparations, excl. diet products” falls under the umbrella of “ALIMENTARY TRACT AND METABOLISM DRUGS,” and it serves as the parent category for “Peripherally acting antiobesity products,” which are defined as antiobesity preparations that act outside the central nervous system. This

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 1427
retrieved_docs.content: Mazindol, setmelanotide, and sibutramine are all classified as centrally acting antiobesity products. Each of these agents belongs to the drug class that exerts its weight‑loss effects by acting on the central nervous system, typically influencing hypothalamic pathways that regulate appetite and energy balance. By being members of the centrally acting antiobesity product category, these medications share a common mechanism of reducing food intake or increasing satiety through central modulation, making them relevant options in the pharmacologic management of obesity.
